stories.thread: The End of an Era (General/Historical Context)
stories.story: The source states, "This is the end. Beautiful friend, the end." For the 1960s, the end arrived with the Rolling Stones' Altamont fiasco in December 1969, the Kent State shootings in May 1970, or Richard Nixon's 1972 re-election. "Mad Men" also signals an "end of an era" starting Sunday, reflecting the 1960s' disintegration. Old orders, like white-shoe WASP firms and the "Good War" generation, were falling apart, replaced by new entities and technologies. This included grimy New York giving way to sunny Los Angeles, and vacuum tubes being displaced by a sleek, solid-state IBM world.
key_facts: The end of the 1960s is associated with Altamont (Dec 1969), Kent State (May 1970), or Nixon's 1972 re-election
"Mad Men" uses the slogan "end of an era" and begins Sunday
"Mad Men" reflects the disintegration of the 1960s
Old orders were falling apart, including WASP firms, New York, "Good War" generation, and old technology
explicit_unknowns: The exact event marking the end of the 1960s depends on one's ideals and tribe.

stories.thread: "Mad Men" Show Overview and Cultural Impact
stories.story: "Mad Men," a show about a 1960s advertising agency, has become part of the national fabric over the past eight years, despite never being a huge ratings hit. Its slogan is "the end of an era," which begins Sunday. The show has inspired fashion lines, cocktails, museum exhibits, and presidential references. Don Draper, the creative director played by Jon Hamm, has become a symbol of the times. In March, a "Mad Men" bench was unveiled in front of New York's Time & Life Building, the fictional headquarters of Sterling Cooper & Partners.
key_facts: "Mad Men" is about a 1960s advertising agency
The show has been on for eight years and is part of the national fabric, but not a huge ratings hit
It has inspired fashion lines, cocktails, museum exhibits, and presidential references
Don Draper (Jon Hamm) is a symbol of the times
A "Mad Men" bench was unveiled in March at New York's Time & Life Building (Sterling Cooper & Partners headquarters)

stories.story: The end of a TV series carries risk, as seen with "The Sopranos," which divided fans with its cut-to-black finale. In contrast, "Breaking Bad" was praised for an almost perfect landing. "Mad Men's" last season, the first half of season 7, concluded with the moon landing in July 1969. Creator Matthew Weiner and the cast have been tight-lipped, with Weiner even hiding the finale from his cast at first. It is reasonable to assume the new season will pick up soon after July 1969, but what will happen is unknown.
key_facts: The end of a TV series brings risk
"The Sopranos" finale divided fans
"Breaking Bad" finale was saluted for an almost perfect landing
"Mad Men" season 7 (first half) ended with the moon landing in July 1969
Matthew Weiner hid the finale from his cast, and he and the cast are tight-lipped
The new season is reasonably assumed to pick up soon after July 1969
explicit_unknowns: What will happen in the new season is unknown.

stories.thread: "Mad Men" Season 7 (Second Half) Plot Guesses - General/Timeline
stories.story: The first half of "Mad Men" season 7 ended with the moon landing in July 1969, and the new season is reasonably assumed to pick up soon afterward. The show probably won't jump ahead much, as the latter half of 1969 offered events like the Manson murders, Woodstock, a New York mayoral campaign, and Vietnam War moratorium demonstrations for characters to interact with. The show might also mention the Miracle Mets as an acknowledgment of the agency's late Lane Pryce. However, Matthew Weiner, who is from Baltimore, might have a different idea, so what will happen is unknown.
key_facts: Season 7 (first half) ended with the moon landing in July 1969
New season is assumed to pick up soon afterward
The show probably won't jump ahead much
Latter half of 1969 included Manson murders, Woodstock, NY mayoral campaign, Vietnam War moratorium demonstrations
The show might mention the Miracle Mets to acknowledge Lane Pryce
explicit_unknowns: What will happen is unknown, as Matthew Weiner might have a different idea.

stories.story: "Mad Men" reflects the disintegration of the 1960s, where old orders like white-shoe WASP firms and the "Good War" generation were falling apart, replaced by new entities and technologies. An ad agency struggles to keep up with these changes. Last season, intraoffice turmoil resulted from the ill-fitting merger between Sterling Cooper and former rival Cutler Gleason and Chaough. The agency survived but is now without Bert Cooper, played by Robert Morse, and owned by McCann Erickson.
key_facts: "Mad Men" is about disintegration, reflecting the 1960s
Old orders are falling apart (e.g., WASP firms, New York, "Good War" generation, old technology)
An ad agency struggles to keep up
Last season had intraoffice turmoil due to Sterling Cooper and Cutler Gleason and Chaough merger
The agency survived but is without Bert Cooper (Robert Morse) and owned by McCann Erickson

stories.thread: "Mad Men" Character Fates - Ken, Harry, Joan
stories.story: The current situation of the agency is not a recipe for long-term survival. It is expected that a number of longtime characters will look for an exit. These characters include Ken Cosgrove, played by Aaron Staton, and Harry Crane, played by Rich Sommer. It is also possible that Joan Harris, played by Christina Hendricks, will seek an exit.
key_facts: The agency's situation is not a recipe for long-term survival
Ken Cosgrove (Aaron Staton) is expected to look for an exit
Harry Crane (Rich Sommer) is expected to look for an exit
explicit_unknowns: Whether Joan Harris (Christina Hendricks) will look for an exit is stated as a possibility ("perhaps").

stories.story: Roger Sterling, the wisecracking executive played by John Slattery, might find an exit, but not one he anticipates. He has suffered two heart attacks, drinks to excess, and has never grown up. A sudden and shocking departure is predicted for him.
key_facts: Roger Sterling (John Slattery) might find an unanticipated exit
He has suffered two heart attacks
He drinks to excess
He has never grown up
A sudden and shocking departure is predicted for him
explicit_unknowns: The exact nature of Roger Sterling's exit is not stated, only that it will be unanticipated, sudden, and shocking.

stories.story: Peggy Olson's star has continued to rise, much like advertising wunderkind Mary Wells Lawrence. She previously left Sterling Cooper and only returned because her new agency merged with her old one. If Olson, played by Elisabeth Moss, bolts the firm again, it will probably be to head her own agency. She might also get married, if she is still interested in such an old-fashioned tradition.
key_facts: Peggy Olson's star has continued to rise (compared to Mary Wells Lawrence)
She left Sterling Cooper once and returned due to a merger
If Peggy Olson (Elisabeth Moss) leaves again, it will probably be to head her own agency
She might get married
explicit_unknowns: Whether Peggy Olson will get married depends on if she is still interested in that old-fashioned tradition.

stories.thread: "Mad Men" Character Fates - Betty Draper/Francis and Sally
stories.story: In recent seasons, Don's ex-wife, Betty, played by January Jones, has lost herself amid turmoil, seeing herself through her spouses' eyes. Her husband, Henry Francis, played by Christopher Stanley, is more supportive than Don but is a busy man. Don's daughter, Sally, played by Kiernan Shipka, is a bright but rebellious teenager. She has run away, sneaked cigarettes, and constantly fights with her mother. It is suggested she could be seen hitchhiking to Woodstock or dropping out of school.
key_facts: Betty (January Jones) has lost herself and sees herself through her spouses' eyes
Henry Francis (Christopher Stanley) is more supportive than Don but busy
Sally (Kiernan Shipka) is bright but a rebellious teenager
Sally has run away, sneaked cigarettes, and fights her mother
It is suggested Sally could hitchhike to Woodstock or drop out of school

stories.thread: "Mad Men" Character Fates - Don Draper (Specific Guess)
stories.story: A specific educated guess for Don Draper's fate suggests it is April 1, 1970, when Richard Nixon signs legislation banning cigarette ads on radio and television. This event reminds Don of the Lucky Strike campaign 10 years earlier, which began the series, a day he had foreseen years prior. Don will fix a drink, ponder buying an avocado-colored refrigerator, clean his ashtray, and leave the show the way he arrived. He will be on top of the zeitgeist, unable to accept his past, and utterly, inscrutably alone.
key_facts: Specific guess for Don Draper's fate is April 1, 1970
Richard Nixon signs legislation banning cigarette ads on radio and television
This reminds Don of the Lucky Strike campaign 10 years earlier
Don had foreseen this day coming years before
Don will fix a drink, ponder an avocado-colored refrigerator, clean his ashtray, and leave the show as he arrived
He will be on top of the zeitgeist, unable to accept his past, and utterly, inscrutably alone
explicit_unknowns: This entire story is presented as an educated guess for Don Draper's fate, not a confirmed plot point.
